_meta.purpose: Per-anomaly Diagnosis-shaped objects used as INPUT to RecommendationAgent.propose() during the PR F eval. Distinct from eval/fixtures/reference-diagnoses.json: that file is the investigation guide the diagnosis judge anchors against; this file is the actual Diagnosis JSON RecommendationAgent consumes. Each diagnosis here is 'sound enough' — a plausible diagnosis the recommendation agent can build on, deliberately not perfect (the recommendation agent shouldn't get bonus signal from over-rich input).
_meta.shape: Matches the canonical Diagnosis type in lib/mcp/types.ts: { conclusion, evidence[], hypothesesConsidered[], affectedCustomers?, confidence?, timeSeries? }.
_meta.currency_note: Olist DB stores monetary amounts in cents (per the seed script). Reais figures cited in the diagnoses below are deliberately rounded to whole Reais so the recommendation agent has clean numbers to ground its impact estimates against.

sp-revenue-drop-w4.conclusion: Sao Paulo (SP) state revenue fell approximately 30% in week 4 of the dataset (~2025-12-22 to 2025-12-29) vs the surrounding 12-week baseline. The drop is isolated to SP — related_segments shows other states (RJ, MG, PR) within normal variance during the same week. The decline is broad across categories within SP rather than driven by a single category collapse, which is consistent with a localized SP demand or fulfillment factor rather than a category-wide shock.
evidence: get_anomaly_context(metric=revenue, dimension=state, segment=SP) returned pct_change=-0.30 for week 4 vs the 12-week baseline.
related_segments showed RJ -2%, MG +1%, PR -3% — no other state moved correlated with SP that week.

get_metric_timeseries(metric=revenue, dimension=category, filter={state:SP}) showed electronics, fashion, and home_decor all between -20% and -35% in SP week 4, with no single category accounting for the bulk of the loss.
Order count in SP also fell ~25% the same week — AOV held roughly flat, so the drop is volume-driven not price-driven.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: Single-category collapse drove the SP revenue drop
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: All examined categories in SP moved together (-20% to -35%); no isolated category collapse.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: Broad demand shock affecting multiple states
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: Other states (RJ, MG, PR) stayed within normal variance — SP is isolated.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: Localized SP fulfillment or demand factor in week 4
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: Isolated to SP, broad across categories, volume-driven (order count fell with revenue) — fits a local-demand or local-fulfillment cause; further investigation by city/carrier needed to narrow it.

affectedCustomers.segmentDescription: SP-state buyers in week 4 of dataset (2025-12-22 to 2025-12-29)

electronics-spike-w2.conclusion: Electronics-category order count spiked approximately 2.5x (around +150%) in week 2 of the dataset (~2025-12-08 to 2025-12-15) vs the 12-week baseline. The spike is broadly distributed across states rather than concentrated in one, and revenue scaled with order count (AOV held roughly stable), so this looks like a real demand surge rather than a vanity-metric AOV artifact. The shape is consistent with a promotional / clearance window or a seasonal pull-forward in mid-December rather than a single large-buyer event.
evidence: get_anomaly_context(metric=order_count, dimension=category, segment=electronics) returned pct_change=+1.50 for week 2 vs the 12-week baseline.
get_metric_timeseries(metric=order_count, dimension=state, filter={category:electronics}) showed SP, RJ, MG, RS, PR all up 100-200% in week 2 — broad geographic distribution.
get_metric_timeseries(metric=revenue, filter={category:electronics}) showed revenue scaling 2.3-2.5x with order count — AOV roughly flat.
related_segments showed no other category moving with electronics that week, suggesting an electronics-specific driver rather than broad seasonality.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: Single large-buyer or bulk-order distorted the metric
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: Order count rose broadly; the spike is distributed across states and many customers, not concentrated.
Other categories did not move with electronics that week.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: Electronics-specific promotional / clearance / pre-holiday demand pull
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: Broad geographic distribution + revenue scaling with volume + isolated to electronics — fits a category-targeted promotional or seasonal event.

affectedCustomers.segmentDescription: Electronics buyers across BR states in week 2 of dataset (2025-12-08 to 2025-12-15)

voucher-dropoff-w10-on.conclusion: Voucher payment volume collapsed to roughly 5% of baseline (~-95%) starting week 10 (~2026-02-02) and remained depressed through the end of the data horizon (week 26, 2026-06-01). Credit_card and boleto volumes did not absorb the lost voucher volume — overall order_count for the affected weeks is also lower, so this is not a clean substitution. The sustained shape across 17 weeks rules out a single-week incident; the timing and persistence are consistent with a voucher-program change (program suspension, expiry, or major rule change) rather than natural decay.
evidence: get_anomaly_context(metric=payment_value, dimension=payment_type, segment=voucher) returned pct_change=-0.95 for weeks 10-26 vs weeks 1-9 baseline.

get_metric_timeseries(metric=payment_value, dimension=payment_type, granularity=week) showed voucher cliff-dropping at week 10 while credit_card and boleto continued near their prior trend — no clean substitution.
Total order_count weeks 10-26 was ~8% below baseline weeks 1-9, indicating part of the voucher volume was lost rather than redirected.
get_metric_timeseries(metric=payment_value, dimension=state, filter={payment_type:voucher}) showed the dropoff is uniform across SP/RJ/MG — not localized.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: Voucher volume substituted to credit_card / boleto
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: Other payment-type volumes did not rise — total order_count fell, so substitution is at best partial.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: Localized cause (single state stopped accepting vouchers)
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: The dropoff is uniform across the top states — not localized.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: Program-level change: voucher program suspended, expired, or restricted starting week 10
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: Cliff-shaped onset at a single week, sustained across 17 weeks, uniform geographically, with partial lost-not-substituted volume — fits a program/policy change.

affectedCustomers.segmentDescription: Customers who previously used voucher payment in weeks 1-9 across BR
